input_text: Autoimmune pancreatic beta-cell destruction in type 1 diabetes mellitus is possibly triggered by an environmental exposure in people who are genetically susceptible to type 1 diabetes mellitus.

Destruction of pancreatic beta-cells in type 1 diabetes mellitus progresses subclinically over months or years until pancreatic beta-cell mass decreases to the point that insulin concentrations are no longer adequate to control plasma glucose levels in patients with type 1 diabetes mellitus.
Type 1 diabetes mellitus generally develops in childhood or adolescence and is the most common form of diabetes mellitus diagnosed before age 20 years.
Type 1 diabetes mellitus can also develop in adults with type 1 diabetes mellitus.

input_text: Susceptibility genes for type 1 diabetes mellitus outside the major histocompatibility complex seem to regulate insulin production and processing and confer risk of type 1 diabetes mellitus in concert with major histocompatibility complex genes.

Susceptibility genes for type 1 diabetes mellitus are more common among some populations than among others and explain the higher prevalence of type 1 diabetes mellitus in people with ancestry from certain areas, such as Scandinavians and Sardinians.
Autoantigens include glutamic acid decarboxylase, insulin, proinsulin, insulinoma-associated protein, zinc transporter ZnT8, and other proteins in beta cells.

input_text: Patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment should change their socks or stockings daily and not wear constricting clothing, such as garters, socks, or stockings with tight elastic tops.
Shoes for patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment should fit well, be wide-toed without open heels or toes, and be changed frequently.

Special shoes should be prescribed for patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment to reduce trauma if the foot is deformed, such as in the case of previous toe amputation, hammer toe, or bunion.

input_text: Many different glucose meters are available for patients to use.
Nearly all glucose meters require test strips and a means for pricking the skin and obtaining a blood sample from patients.
The choice among glucose meter devices is usually based on patient preferences for features such as time to results, which usually ranges from 5 to 30 seconds.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for features such as size of display panel, with large screens benefiting patients with poor eyesight.

input_text: The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for features such as voice readout, which benefits patients with visual impairment.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for features such as smartphone app connectivity.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for features such as availability, affordability, and insurance coverage of test strips for patients.
Continuous glucose monitoring systems estimate capillary blood glucose from interstitial glucose detected by a subcutaneous sensor in patients.

Continuous glucose monitoring systems can either provide glucose measurements continuously, known as real-time continuous glucose monitoring, or intermittently when scanned with a device, known as intermittently scanned continuous glucose monitoring.

Immune dysfunction is another major long-term complication of type 1 diabetes.
